channel.title: Darkhorse Press - Top Story
channel.description: Latest Top Story posts from Darkhorse Press.

item.title: UPDATE: Starkville officer remains in critical condition after funeral escort crash - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
STARKVILLE — A Starkville Police Department motorcycle officer remains in critical condition after being injured in a crash while assisting with a funeral procession escort, according to an update released Sunday by the department.

In the Feb. 23 update, police said they were following up after receiving strong community support and inquiries about the officer’s condition. The department said it is grateful for the “overwhelming support, prayers, and encouragement” from the local community, across Mississippi and from out of state.

Police said the officer remains in critical condition and asked the public to continue praying for the officer, the officer’s family and the Starkville Police Department.

According to the department’s original release, the crash happened Saturday, Feb. 21, at about 1:43 p.m. while the officer was assisting with a funeral procession escort.

Police said the department motorcycle and another vehicle, which was not part of the procession, collided. The officer suffered serious injuries and was flown to a medical facility in Jackson for treatment.

The driver of the other vehicle remained at the scene and declined medical treatment, police said.

The Starkville Police Department Accident Reconstruction Team is conducting a full reconstruction of the crash, and the collision remains under investigation.

item.title: STEPPING UP TO SERVE: MHP donates two patrol SUV's to Jefferson Davis County Sheriff's Department - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
JEFFERSON DAVIS COUNTY – Jefferson Davis County Sheriff Ron Strickland and the Jefferson Davis County Sheriff’s Office are thanking the Mississippi Highway Patrol and Trooper Victor Mays for donating two patrol SUVs to the department.

In a statement, the sheriff’s office said the donation will help deputies better serve and protect the community.

The department also thanked the Mississippi Highway Patrol for its continued support of public safety and for assisting fellow law enforcement agencies across the county and state.

Sheriff’s officials said partnerships between agencies help strengthen law enforcement efforts in Mississippi.

item.title: Zero-turn mower stolen from Seminary Youth Association Ball Field in Covington County - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
COVINGTON COUNTY — The Covington County Sheriff’s Office is asking for the public’s help identifying a vehicle and its occupants in connection with the theft of a 72-inch Big Dog zero-turn mower from the Seminary Youth Association Ball Field.

According to information released by the sheriff’s office, the theft occurred Feb. 19, 2026, at the ball field in Seminary.

Investigators said a vehicle arrived at the location at about 1:40 p.m. without a trailer, then returned at about 3:21 p.m. with a trailer. Authorities said the mower was stolen during the later visit.

The sheriff’s office is asking anyone who recognizes the vehicle or its occupants, or who saw the vehicle in the area, to contact its confidential tips line at 601-641-4618.

item.title: UMMC clinics to remain closed Monday, Tuesday - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
By Patrice Guilfoyle University of Mississippi Medical Center

JACKSON – The University of Mississippi Medical Center clinics will remain closed statewide Monday and Tuesday.

Medical Center teams continue to respond to the Feb. 19 cyberattack alongside federal and state agencies and national experts in cybersecurity. Our phone systems and ability to receive or send emails remain down or unreliable.

The UMMC Emergency Departments and hospitals in Jackson, Madison County, Grenada and Holmes County are open and caring for patients. The Medical Center is actively working on a solution for patients who need to contact us with routine medical and/or medication needs.

UMMC has contacted patients receiving ongoing time-sensitive care to arrange treatment.

“Our care teams continue to provide exceptional service to our patients and families. I know this intrusion into our system has negatively impacted some of our patients, but please know that we are using every resource at our disposal to resolve this issue in a safe and effective manner,” said Dr. LouAnn Woodward, vice chancellor for health affairs.

item.title: Two Vicksburg Homicide Suspects Arrested in Indiana - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
VICKSBURG — Two 18-year-old suspects wanted in connection with a Vicksburg homicide were arrested Sunday in Kokomo, Indiana, authorities said.

According to Kokomo police, officers were contacted by the Vicksburg Police Department on Feb. 21 regarding information that the suspects might be in the Kokomo area.

Investigators located the suspects’ vehicle at a residence east of the city. Around 8 a.m. Feb. 22, officers executed a search warrant at the home. After a brief negotiation, two suspects were taken into custody on first-degree murder charges.

Police identified the suspects as Quintin Wilson and Arkeyveion Stamps, both 18.

Vicksburg police said Wilson and Stamps are wanted in connection with the death of Jaylon Walker, 18, of Hermanville. Walker was found dead inside his vehicle on U.S. Highway 61 South in Vicksburg on Feb. 11.

Wilson and Stamps are being held in Indiana pending extradition to Mississippi.

item.title: State Fire Officials to Host Virtual Town Hall, Announce Major Funding for Departments - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
JACKSON — Mississippi fire service leaders and personnel are invited to participate in a statewide virtual town hall Monday morning that will include major funding announcements, policy updates and a live question-and-answer session with program leaders.

The “MSFIRE Virtual Town Hall & Open Office Hours” is scheduled for 9 a.m. Feb. 23 and will be hosted by the state fire coordinator and MSFIRE. Organizers say the session is designed for fire chiefs, coordinators, officers, volunteers, career personnel and partner agencies across Mississippi.

Officials plan to release details during the session about millions of dollars in funding available for Mississippi fire departments.

The event will feature real-time statewide updates from the Mississippi Forestry Commission, the Mississippi Firefighters Association and state fire leadership, according to organizers.

Among the featured topics:

Organizers said additional statewide updates and open discussion will be included as time allows.

The virtual session is open to anyone connected to Mississippi’s fire service. Registration is available online.

item.title: Sheriff Urges Residents to Use Crime Stoppers to Help Fight Crime - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
JACKSON — Jackson Police Chief and Hinds County Sheriff Tyree D. Jones is urging residents to report criminal activity through Central Mississippi Crime Stoppers, emphasizing that public safety depends on community involvement.

In a Facebook post late last week, Jones said fighting crime is not solely the responsibility of law enforcement but a shared duty among residents.

“The safety of our community is not the responsibility of law enforcement alone,” Jones said. “It belongs to all of us. Every tip called in, every piece of information shared, and every neighbor who speaks up makes our streets safer for the families we serve.”

Jones encouraged residents to use Central Mississippi Crime Stoppers, which allows individuals to submit tips anonymously.

“You do not have to give your name. You just have to care,” Jones said. “If you know something, say something. Together, we are stronger than crime.”

Crime Stoppers accepts tips by phone at 601-355-TIPS (8477) and online at p3tips.com .

item.title: Police Investigating Four Threats Against Pearl Daycares - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
PEARL — Authorities are investigating four threats made against daycares in the city of Pearl since last week, officials said.

Investigators with the Pearl Police Department, working in coordination with the Mississippi Department of Homeland Security, are attempting to identify the source of the threats.

Police said they have not been able to verify the credibility of any of the threats at this time.

Out of an abundance of caution, both uniformed and non-uniformed Pearl police officers will increase patrols around local daycare centers to help ensure the safety of children and staff.

“The safety of our kids remains our top priority, and we will continue to take every necessary step to protect them,” officials said in a statement.

Authorities did not release additional details about the nature of the threats. The investigation remains ongoing.

item.title: Panola County Man Arrested in Armed Robbery at Dollar General - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
PANOLA COUNTY — A 19-year-old man has been arrested in connection with an armed robbery at a Dollar General store in western Panola County, authorities said.

With assistance from Crime Stoppers of Panola County and the Quitman County Sheriff’s Office, investigators arrested Nequan Ballentine, 19, in connection with the robbery, according to the Panola County Sheriff’s Office.

The robbery occurred around 10 p.m. at the Dollar General located at McDowell Road and Highway 6. Authorities said an armed Black male wearing a hoodie and face covering entered the store at closing time and forced employees to open the safe. The suspect took money from the safe before fleeing in a gray-colored car westbound on Highway 6.

No injuries were reported.

Investigators initially asked anyone with information about the crime to contact Crime Stoppers of Panola County.

Ballentine’s charges and additional details about the arrest were not immediately released. The investigation remains ongoing.

item.title: Officials Urge Caution as Wildfires Burn Across South Mississippi - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
PERRY COUNTY — Over the weekend, fire officials were urging residents to avoid outdoor burning and use extreme caution as dangerous fire conditions contributed to wildfires across south and central Mississippi.

No formal burn bans are currently in place, but the danger is real, authorities said.

A wildfire burned an estimated 500-acre area near Paret Tower Road in Perry County this weekend, authorities said. Law enforcement issued evacuation orders for nearby areas and assisted residents as flames threatened homes and other structures.

The wildfire comes as the National Weather Service warns that a combination of gusty winds, somewhat dry fuels and low relative humidity is creating significant fire danger. A Red Flag Warning remains in effect for portions of south Mississippi and northeast Louisiana along the Highway 84 corridor, where fuels are especially dry.

The Weather Service said open burning should not be attempted in the warning area because wildfires can spread rapidly. Farther north, officials said outdoor burning is discouraged because fires are more likely to escape control.

Fire service officials ask residents to use caution when burning debris and to pay close attention to weather conditions, noting that high winds can significantly increase the risk of losing control of a fire.

While there are currently no county-issued burn bans in effect, burn bans must be enacted by county boards of supervisors and are not automatically triggered when hazardous conditions develop.

Authorities said residents should not interpret the absence of a burn ban as an indication that conditions are safe. Instead, they are asking the public to avoid open burning during periods of high wind and low humidity and to follow evacuation orders and updates from local officials as conditions evolve.

Multiple agencies responded to the Perry County blaze, including the Mississippi Forestry Commission, the Beaumont Volunteer Fire Department and the Carnes Volunteer Fire Department, along with local law enforcement and other first responders.

Interstate 12 was shut down in both directions between Highway 59 and Highway 434 because of active forest fires.

The cause of the Perry County fire remains under investigation.

In Copiah County on Sunday morning, a debris burn grew out of control on Barnes Road, leading to a brush fire that destroyed about 50 acres of grass and brush, according to the Crystal Springs Volunteer Fire Department.

Officials told reporters the fire started around 10:30 a.m. when a homeowner was burning debris. High winds quickly pushed the flames beyond the homeowner’s control.

item.title: MSU hosts institute students helping solve statewide, global challenges - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
STARKVILLE — High school students from across the Magnolia State convened at Mississippi State Friday as participants of the eighth-annual World Food Prize Mississippi Youth Institute, hosted by MSU’s College of Agriculture and Life Sciences.

The Mississippi Youth Institute provides high school students the opportunity to research and suggest solutions for some of the most pressing global issues, including food security, water quality and health, said acting CALS Dean and institute leader Darrell Sparks.

“These are our future leaders, so it is important that they learn to problem solve and effectively communicate their ideas with others,” he said. “This program provides a constructive space for them to network with their peers as well as experts from industry and academia.”

The institute included the Charles E. Lindley Lecture featuring Ryn Laster, director of food safety and animal welfare at Cal-Maine Foods Inc. headquartered in Ridgeland. She is a four-time MSU graduate and has spent her career ensuring the highest standards in egg production, quality assurance and sustainable practices that support public health and industry integrity. Her presentation emphasized the critical roles of science, policy and leadership in strengthening food systems. After the presentation, students participated in preparing meal packs for distribution to state food pantries.

Participating students were recognized as Borlaug Scholars, with select students invited to participate in the Global Youth Institute in Iowa in October. Participants are eligible for CALS scholarships and can apply to the Borlaug Ruan International Internship and the USDA Wallace-Carver Fellowship. The World Food Prize Global Youth Institute was created by 1970 Nobel Peace Prize winner Norman Borlaug, who spent his life’s work finding solutions to feed a hungry world.

Last year, Greenville Christian School’s Jaquarius Harvey was selected to attend the GYI. He has participated in the program for three years and shared his experience with this year’s attendees.

“Even though I grew up in a rural community, I didn’t know how I could fit into agriculture,” he said. “World Food Prize has shown me how I can use my unique skills to help others. After graduation, I plan to obtain an engineering degree and apply it in agricultural development.”

He encouraged anyone interested in the program to “get outside your comfort zone.”

“I’ve been able to experience so many new things, like speaking in front of larger crowds and even flying for the first time,” he said. “You never know what opportunities might come your way.”

This year’s World Food Prize Mississippi Youth Institute Borlaug Scholars are (by school):

The Mississippi Youth Institute is hosted by MSU with generous support from CALS alumni Barry L. and Lana B. White and the Madison Charitable Foundation. To learn more, visit www.worldfoodprize.org/en/youth_programs/global_youth_institute/mississippi.

For more on MSU’s College of Agriculture and Life Sciences, visit www.cals.msstate.edu .

Mississippi State University is taking care of what matters. Learn more at www.msstate.edu .

item.title: Monday Weather: Fire danger today! - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Mary Apel
item.description:
The combination of breezy northwest winds, somewhat dry fuels, and low relative humidity values will lead to significant fire danger concerns persisting today.

After a chilly start to the week, another warm up is expected mid & late week, with a cold front currently forecast to affect the area Thursday. Quiet weather exists through Wednesday, but a cold front brings increasing chances for rain Thursday into Thursday night, with quieter conditions expected Friday into the weekend.

item.title: MDOC inmates complete financial literacy boot camp at Madison County work center - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
MADISON COUNTY — The Mississippi Department of Corrections said inmates at the Madison County Community Work Center recently completed a financial literacy boot camp aimed at helping participants build money management skills.

MDOC said the class was offered through a partnership with the Mississippi Community Financial Access Coalition and Guaranty Bank.

The department said 24 participants took part in the program. Inmate Earvin Boglin told fellow participants that saving money requires time, self-discipline and patience, and said he learned the importance of avoiding high-cost borrowing such as payday loans.

MDOC also said inmate Colby Reed encouraged fellow inmates to stay positive and keep faith while working toward a better future.

The department said several participants, including Boglin and Reed, were featured in photos with community work center staff following the class.

item.title: MDAC Accepting Proposals for Specialty Crop Block Grant Program - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
JACKSON — The Mississippi Department of Agriculture and Commerce is accepting proposals for the Specialty Crop Block Grant Program, Commissioner Andy Gipson announced.

The program, funded by the U.S. Department of Agriculture and authorized by the Agriculture Improvement Act of 2018, aims to enhance the competitiveness of Mississippi’s specialty crop industry.

The grants support efforts to market and promote specialty crops, assist producers with research and development, expand access and availability, and address local, regional and national challenges facing specialty crop producers.

Specialty crops include fruits and vegetables, tree nuts, dried fruits, horticulture and nursery crops, including floriculture. According to MDAC, specialty crops accounted for more than $201 million of Mississippi’s agriculture industry in 2025.

Past projects funded through the program have included garden grants to increase awareness and consumption of specialty crops among school-aged children; marketing campaigns promoting locally grown products; food safety improvements through Good Agricultural Practices; research into new crop varieties; honey bee colony health initiatives; grower education seminars and trainings; optical imaging technology for sweet potato and blueberry packing lines; and high tunnel research trials for fruits, vegetables and herbs.

Eligible applicants include producer groups and associations, nonprofits, colleges and universities. Funding will not be awarded for projects that solely benefit a single commercial product or provide profit to one organization, institution or individual, though individual entities may participate as project partners.

Applications must be submitted to MDAC by April 1, 2026. Application materials are available in the “Alerts and Announcements” section of mdac.ms.gov, by calling Susan Lawrence at 601-213-7542 or by emailing [email protected] .

item.title: Man Fatally Shot During Domestic Disturbance on Lake Glen Drive, Police Say - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
JACKSON — A 35-year-old man was fatally shot early Sunday during a reported domestic disturbance at a residence in Jackson, authorities said.

Officers with the Jackson Police Department’s Precinct 3 responded about 7:21 a.m. to the 600 block of Lake Glen Drive after receiving reports of a disturbance. When officers arrived, they learned that shots had been fired at the home.

Inside the residence, officers found the body of a man lying at the front door of the house. He was pronounced dead at the scene.

Police later identified the victim as Michael D. Monger, 35. His next of kin has been notified.

Several individuals were inside the home at the time of the shooting, according to police. Weapons and shell casings were recovered from the scene.

The department’s Robbery/Homicide Unit and Mobile Crime Unit responded to assist with the investigation, along with the Hinds County Sheriff’s Office coroner.

Multiple people were transported to Jackson Police Department headquarters for questioning by homicide detectives. As of Sunday, no arrests had been made.

The investigation remains ongoing. Anyone with information is asked to contact the Jackson Police Department at 601-960-1234 or Crime Stoppers at 601-355-TIPS (8477).

Officers with the Jackson Police Department’s Precinct 3 responded about 7:21 a.m. to the 600 block of Lake Glen Drive after receiving reports of a disturbance. When officers arrived, they learned that shots had been fired at the home.

Inside the residence, officers found the body of a man lying at the front door of the house. He was pronounced dead at the scene.

Police later identified the victim as Michael D. Monger, 35. His next of kin has been notified.

item.title: Elderly Woman Killed in Jackson House Fire - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
JACKSON – A 78-year-old woman lost her life in a house fire in Jackson Monday evening.

According to Hinds County Coroner Jeramiah Howard, the fire broke out at a home on Ferncrest Drive. He received a call at 5:34 p.m.

The victim is described as a 78-year-old female who lived at the address. The Jackson Fire Department is working to determine the cause of teh fire.

The coroner will release her identity once other friends and family are notified.

item.title: Early Morning Fire Damages Units at Walker Hill Apartments in Meridian - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Cash Matlock
item.description:
MERIDIAN – A structure fire early Monday morning damaged multiple units at the Walker Hill Apartments in Meridian, according to fire officials.

The fire was reported around 5 a.m. at the apartment complex located near 21st Street and Old Marion Road.

Meridian Fire Chief Michael Evans said one unit was heavily damaged by the blaze, and the roof of a neighboring unit also sustained damage.

Firefighters evacuated residents from surrounding apartments as a precaution. No injuries were reported.

Officials said the fire is believed to have started in a bedroom. The cause remains under investigation.

item.title: Community diaper drive planned March 14 in Booneville - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
BOONEVILLE – The Northeast Mississippi Healthy Start Program and Consortium, in collaboration with the Mississippi State University Extension Center, will host a Community Diaper Drive on Saturday, March 14, in Booneville.

The event is scheduled for 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. at the Prentiss County Agri-Center, located at 2301 North Second Street.

Organizers said the event will provide free diapers, wipes and hygiene essentials to the community. A crib raffle also is planned.

For more information, organizers listed Katherine Hickman and Ray Shoemaker as contacts. Email contacts on the flyer are [email protected] and [email protected] .

item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
BATESVILLE – With very few details, the Batesville Police Department posted a photo of a man and asked if anyone can identify him.

It is unclear why he they are seeking his identity.

If you recognize him, please call Batesville police at 662-563-5653.

item.title: Starkville Officer Critically Injured in Crash During Funeral Escort - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
STARKVILLE — A Starkville police officer was critically injured Friday afternoon in a crash while assisting with a funeral procession escort, authorities said.

The collision occurred just before 1:45 p.m. Feb. 21, according to the Starkville Police Department.

Police said a motorcycle officer who was part of the funeral procession escort collided with a vehicle that was not involved in the procession. The impact left the officer with serious injuries.

The officer was airlifted to a hospital in Jackson for further treatment and was listed in critical condition, police said.

The driver of the other vehicle remained at the scene and declined medical treatment.

The cause of the crash remains under investigation.

item.title: UMMC says FBI teams assisting onsite as cyberattack response continues - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
JACKSON – The University of Mississippi Medical Center said Friday that teams are working “around the clock” to respond to a cyberattack that disrupted systems Thursday, with federal authorities and specialized FBI teams assisting onsite.

Cybersecurity attack forces UMMC to close statewide clinics, cancel outpatient procedures

In an update dated Feb. 20, 2026, UMMC said it has also engaged three national vendors specializing in cyber forensics, recovery and security. The medical center said it remains unknown how extensive the intrusion is or how long it will take to return to normal operations.

UMMC said its phone systems and its ability to send or receive emails remain down or unreliable.

Hospital services and emergency departments continue operating, UMMC said, adding that it has notified other hospitals it can again accept patient transfers requiring higher levels of care. UMMC directed facilities seeking transfers to contact Mississippi MED-COM at 601-362-4264 and said ambulance services have been notified that the medical center is no longer on diversion.

UMMC said it is developing a way for patients to contact the medical center regarding routine medical or medication needs and will announce that process once available. The medical center said it is also reaching out to patients who need time-sensitive treatments, including chemotherapy, to arrange appointments.

UMMC apologized for the disruption and said it hopes to return to normal operations as soon as possible.

item.title: Two Arrested in Madison Auto Burglaries; Third Suspect Sought - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
MADISON — Two Jackson men have been arrested and charged in connection with a series of vehicle burglaries reported across Madison, and authorities are searching for a third suspect.

The Madison Police Department said officers responded Thursday, Feb. 19, to reports of multiple auto burglaries throughout the city.

Investigators quickly developed leads that led them to Jackson, where a vehicle of interest was located on Maria Drive. With assistance from the Mississippi Capitol Police and the Hinds County Sheriff’s Department, authorities arrested 25-year-old Willie Deonta Thomas and 23-year-old Harold Stiff, both of Jackson.

Police said evidence connected to the burglaries was recovered during the investigation.

Both men have been charged with eight counts of auto burglary. Additional charges are possible.

Authorities also issued a warrant for Christian B. Stiff of Jackson on eight counts of auto burglary and are seeking information that could lead to his arrest.

During the investigation, officers determined Thomas was on parole from the Mississippi Department of Corrections at the time of his arrest. Investigators also said Harold Stiff is currently out on bond in Hinds County on an aggravated assault charge.

The suspects are scheduled for an initial appearance in City of Madison Municipal Court. The case will be forwarded to District Attorney Bubba Bramlett for prosecution.

item.title: Traffic stop in Picayune leads to drug arrests after deputies find meth, pills - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
PICAYUNE — Two men were arrested following a traffic stop in Picayune after narcotics investigators reported finding methamphetamine and prescription pills inside a vehicle, according to the Pearl River County Sheriff’s Department.

Sheriff’s officials said narcotics investigators stopped a 2005 Infiniti QX56 on Ceasar Road on Wednesday, Feb. 4, 2026, for disregard of traffic devices.

Authorities identified the driver as James Spence, 43, of Carriere, and the passenger as Cameron Franks, 20, also of Carriere. Investigators said Spence was arrested during the stop for having no driver’s license.

During an inventory of the vehicle, investigators reported locating methamphetamine, Adderall and oxycodone. Both Spence and Franks were taken into custody, the sheriff’s department said.

Spence was charged with felony possession with intent to distribute, felony possession of controlled substances and misdemeanor possession of controlled substances, according to the department. Franks was charged with felony possession of controlled substances.

Sheriff David Allison credited deputies and investigators for their work in drug enforcement efforts in the county.

item.title: Second suspect arrested in Scheffel Road ATV theft case in Adams County - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
ADAMS COUNTY – A second suspect has been arrested in an ongoing investigation into the theft and recovery of an all-terrain vehicle from property on Scheffel Road, according to the Adams County Sheriff’s Office.

Sheriff’s officials said Jerry W. Rodgers Jr., 49, of Natchez, was taken into custody Friday, Feb. 20, 2026, at about 7:54 a.m. He was charged with receiving, possessing, or disposing of stolen property.

Rodgers was booked into the Adams County Jail, and bond was set at $60,000.

Authorities said the arrest follows the recovery of the stolen ATV and the earlier arrest of Amanda Breland on related charges. Investigators said the case remains active and additional arrests are possible.

Anyone with information is asked to contact the Adams County Sheriff’s Office at 601-442-2752 or Adams County E911.

item.title: Pedestrian hit by vehicle in Tupelo - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
TUPELO — Tupelo police were responding Friday to a vehicle-versus-pedestrian crash on North Gloster Street near McCullough Boulevard.

The Tupelo Police Department said an adult pedestrian was injured and transported to North Mississippi Medical Center for treatment. The extent of the injuries was not released.

Police urged motorists to use caution in the area and consider alternate routes while emergency crews worked the scene.

Authorities said additional information may be released as it becomes available.

item.title: National Caregivers Day Shines Light on Millions Providing Unseen, Unpaid Care - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
USA — National Caregivers Day, observed the third Friday in February, honors the millions of Americans who provide daily care to loved ones, patients and residents, often with little recognition and, in most cases, no pay at all.

The observance was established in 2015 by the Providers Association for Home Health & Hospice Agencies, known as PAHHHA, according to NationalToday.com. The first celebration took place in 2016, with the goal of dedicating a day each year to recognize and appreciate caregivers everywhere.

Caregivers provide everything from medical aid to personal grooming, feeding, and providing comfort and companionship. Yet across the board, many are underappreciated, underpaid or unpaid.

The scope of caregiving in the United States is vast. The National Alliance for Caregiving and AARP reported that in 2020, an estimated 53 million Americans provided unpaid care to an adult or child. One in five U.S. adults offers unpaid care to an adult family member, according to surveys cited by Radiant Senior Living .

The Gallup-Healthways Well-Being Index found caregivers spend an average of six days each month grooming, feeding, dressing, bathing and walking those in their care. They spend 13 days a month commuting, cleaning, doing laundry, monitoring medication, shopping and preparing special meals. Another 13 hours monthly are devoted to coordinating doctor visits, researching symptoms and managing finances, NationalToday.com reports.

Those are black and white averages in articles written on the internet. To many Americans, it’s 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, 365 days a year.

Women shoulder much of that responsibility. The Institute on Aging reports that more than three-quarters of caregivers are female and spend 50% more time providing essential services than men. Nearly 75% of caregivers are women, according to data cited by RadiantSeniorLiving.com.

Caregiving can take a toll. Per the National Alliance for Caregiving, 23% of Americans say caregiving has harmed their health. Nearly 40% of caregivers over age 50 describe the emotional strain as highly demanding, and 42% report a financial impact from their caregiving responsibilities.

Many caregivers are older adults themselves. NationalToday.com reports the average age of someone caring for a person 65 or older is 63. As the number of hours devoted to caregiving increases, many report their own health challenges.

Professional caregivers also face demanding schedules and emotional labor. Radiant Senior Living notes that professional caregivers “frequently treat their patients and residents as if they were members of their own families,” providing consistent care and compassion.

National Caregivers Day recognizes both professional and informal caregivers, including friends, family members, pastors, and even surviving parents navigating grief while supporting children who have lost a parent.

Psychology Today highlights the unique challenges facing grieving caregivers, especially surviving parents.

“The well-being of a caregiver who is grieving is essential to their child’s well-being,” Rebecca Hobbs-Lawrence, M.A., of the Dougy Center for Grieving Children & Families, told the publication. “Caregivers need space to grieve themselves and to seek the support of others.”

She said it is helpful for caregivers to dedicate time to their own thoughts and feelings, whether through walking, journaling, making art or simply sitting quietly.

Experts and advocacy groups say small gestures can make a significant difference. Writing a positive review, sending a handwritten thank-you note, offering respite care, assisting with errands or simply acknowledging a caregiver’s efforts are meaningful ways to show appreciation.

For that son or daughter who is caring for their ailing parent, which is physically, financially, personally, spiritually, and emotionally devastating, simply taking them to lunch or coffee, listening to them talk about what they’re going through, and letting them know they’re seen and not alone is a priceless gift.

NationalToday.com notes that caregivers’ “special kindness and patience” deserve year-round recognition. On National Caregivers Day, supporters are encouraged to check in, offer help and remember that caring for the caregiver is just as important as caring for those they serve.

item.title: Mississippi’s Fire on the Forty Initiative Receives National Land Stewardship Award - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
JACKSON — Mississippi’s Fire on the Forty Initiative has received the Land Stewardship Award from the National Wild Turkey Federation, state wildlife officials announced.

The award, which honors outstanding efforts to promote wildlife habitat management, was presented during the federation’s 50th annual Convention and Sport Show in Nashville.

The initiative, led by the Mississippi Department of Wildlife, Fisheries, and Parks, is designed to expand the use of prescribed fire on privately owned fields and upland forests. The program provides hands-on workshops for landowners and offers cost-sharing assistance to help apply prescribed burns.

“We are honored to see Fire on the Forty recognized for its impact on wildlife habitat across Mississippi,” said MDWFP Executive Director Lynn Posey. “We look forward to seeing the continued impact prescribed burning has on Mississippi.”

The Fire on the Forty program is funded through multiple sources, including the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service’s Partners for Fish and Wildlife Program, the National Fish and Wildlife Foundation, the National Wild Turkey Federation and the Mississippi Forestry Commission.

The initiative is administered by a committee of resource professionals from MDWFP, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, Mississippi State University, Wildlife Mississippi, the Mississippi Forestry Commission, the National Wild Turkey Federation and the Mississippi Wildlife, Fisheries, Parks Marine Foundation.

“This award reflects the strong partnerships and dedicated landowners who make Fire on the Forty possible, and the lasting benefits prescribed fire provides for wildlife habitat and forest health across Mississippi,” said John Gruchy, MDWFP private lands program coordinator.

More information about the program is available at mdwfp.com/privatelands .

item.title: Kemper County sheriff seeks public’s help locating man wanted on aggravated assault charge - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
KEMPER COUNTY – The Kemper County Sheriff’s Office is asking for the public’s help locating a man wanted in connection with an aggravated assault case.

Authorities identified the suspect as Roosevelt Daniels. The sheriff’s office said Daniels is wanted on an aggravated assault charge and urged anyone with information about his whereabouts to contact investigators.

Anyone with information is asked to call the Kemper County Sheriff’s Office at 601-743-2255.

item.title: Judge sentences man to 20 years in Pontotoc County meth trafficking case - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
PONTOTOC COUNTY – A Pontotoc County man has been sentenced to 20 years in prison after pleading guilty to trafficking methamphetamine, according to the First Circuit District Attorney’s Office.

Prosecutors said Adrian Rashad McCoy entered the guilty plea Feb. 19, 2026. McCoy had been indicted by a Pontotoc County grand jury on a charge of trafficking of methamphetamine, officials said.

Judge Kelly Mims sentenced McCoy to 20 years in the custody of the Mississippi Department of Corrections, the district attorney’s office said. Prosecutors said McCoy will be required to serve the full sentence and is not eligible for parole or early release.

The case was investigated by Kory Clowers, the Pontotoc County Sheriff’s Department and the North Mississippi Narcotics Unit, according to the district attorney’s office. Assistant District Attorneys Megan French and Sidra Winter prosecuted the case.

item.title: Jones County deputies seek woman suspected of taking black 2016 Volkswagen Jetta - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
JONES COUNTY — The Jones County Sheriff’s Department is asking for the public’s help locating a woman investigators describe as the primary suspect in the felony taking of a motor vehicle.

Investigators said Lisa Crews is suspected of taking a black 2016 Volkswagen Jetta from a residence on Jeff Byrd Road in eastern Jones County. The vehicle is described as displaying a Jones County disabled tag, DB 5896R, authorities said.

Deputies said anyone with information about Crews’ whereabouts or the location of the vehicle is asked to contact the Jones County Sheriff’s Department at 601-425-3147 or Jones County Crime Stoppers at 601-428-STOP (7867). Officials said tips can be made anonymously.

The sheriff’s department said a booking photo of Crews from 2018 was released, noting that her appearance may have changed. Authorities also shared a stock photo of a black 2016 Volkswagen Jetta, emphasizing it is not the actual victim’s vehicle.

item.title: Jackson Police Seek Suspects in Early-Morning Vehicle Burglaries - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
JACKSON — Jackson Police Department is asking for the public’s help identifying suspects linked to a series of vehicle burglaries earlier this month.

Police said several vehicles were broken into during the early morning hours of Feb. 11 in the 5600 block of Keele Street and the 200 block of Briarwood Drive.

According to investigators, the suspects entered multiple vehicles and stole personal property. Authorities have released still images of individuals captured in the area at the time of the incidents.

The investigation remains ongoing.

Anyone who recognizes the individuals or has surveillance footage from the area is urged to contact the Jackson Police Department or submit an anonymous tip through Crime Stoppers.

Residents are also reminded to lock their vehicles and remove valuables to help prevent theft.

item.title: Hattiesburg police arrest armed robbery suspect hiding in attic after hours-long standoff - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
HATTIESBURG — Hattiesburg police arrested a 32-year-old man on an armed robbery charge after a standoff Friday in the 300 block of East Scooba Street.

Officers responded about 10:30 a.m. Feb. 20 while attempting to locate Brandon Rice, 32, of Hattiesburg, who police said was wanted in connection with an armed robbery reported about 9 a.m. in the 900 block of Broadway Drive.

Detectives identified Rice as the suspect and believed he was inside a residence on East Scooba Street, police said. Officers attempted to make contact for more than two hours before entering the home.

During a search, Rice was found hiding in an attic and was taken into custody without incident at about 2:10 p.m., police said.

Rice was charged with one count of armed robbery. Police said the investigation remains ongoing.

item.title: “Great Jones County Donkey Roundup” ends with three donkeys in safe custody - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
JONES COUNTY — The Jones County Sheriff’s Department is asking for the public’s help locating the owner of three male donkeys found roaming near Quit Graves Road and Forest Holifield Road.

In a social media post, the sheriff’s department said deputies and residents tracked down the donkeys during what officials jokingly dubbed the “Great Jones County Donkey Roundup.” The department said Lee Stringer, Sam Smith, David Hare and Deputy R.H. Stockman helped take the animals into custody.

The sheriff’s department said the donkeys are now being cared for, fed and sheltered by Stringer while officials work to identify the owner.

Anyone who owns the three donkeys is asked to contact Stringer at 601-670-6345 to arrange pickup.

item.title: Genealogical DNA Identifies ‘Natchez John Doe’ as Kentucky Man - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
NATCHEZ — Even as national headlines focus on genealogical DNA in the disappearance of Nancy Guthrie, skeletal remains discovered after a routine traffic stop in Natchez two years ago have been identified through similar means.

The set of remains found in March of 2024 were identified as a Kentucky man through advanced DNA testing and forensic genetic genealogy, according to Othram Labs.

The remains, long known as “Natchez John Doe,” were identified as William Francis Harrington after scientists developed a comprehensive DNA profile and confirmed a close biological relationship with a potential relative, Othram said.

The case began in 2024 when two Natchez police officers conducted a traffic stop near the intersection of Sgt. Prentiss Drive and Fatherland Road, according to previous reporting by WLBT and statements from Natchez Police Department Commander Jerry Ford.

Ford told reporters that officers noticed drug paraphernalia inside the small pickup truck after making contact with the driver.

“The officer who was backing up went up to the driver door, made contact with the driver and noticed paraphernalia inside of his vehicle,” Ford said.

After the driver was detained, he told officers he had information about a corpse in the nearby area.

“The suspect at that time stated to him that he had information about a corpse in the nearby area,” Ford said.

Officers called for backup and searched a wooded area about 10 feet off the road between Fatherland Road and the turnoff to First Lutheran Church. Beneath fallen leaves, they discovered skeletal remains buried in a shallow grave about 10 feet into the woods.

The remains were largely intact, but the head was missing. Authorities said the body had been there for a significant amount of time. At the time, no missing persons reports appeared to match the victim’s identity, and investigators declined to speculate about the detainee’s potential role or knowledge.

The Mississippi State Medical Examiner’s Office determined the remains belonged to an adult male estimated to be between 5 feet 9 inches and 6 feet 4 inches tall. An identification card found nearby suggested the man could have been around 70 years old, but officials could not confirm his identity. The case was entered into the National Missing and Unidentified Persons System as UP124871.

In June 2024, the Medical Examiner’s Office partnered with Othram, a forensic laboratory based in The Woodlands, Texas, to apply advanced DNA testing. According to Othram Labs, scientists used Forensic-Grade Genome Sequencing® to develop a DNA profile and conducted a forensic genetic genealogy search that generated new investigative leads.

Investigators later collected a reference DNA sample from a possible relative. Using Othram’s KinSNP® rapid relationship testing, scientists confirmed a close relationship, leading to the identification of the remains as Harrington, who was from Kentucky.

Othram said the advanced DNA testing and genealogy work in the case were funded through donations made via its DNASolves® crowdfunding platform.

The company said the identification marks the 38th publicly announced case in Mississippi in which officials have used Othram’s identity inference technology to help name unidentified remains.

item.title: Fitch Urges Passage of Gun Crime Bills Targeting Juvenile Offenders, Gang Activity - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
JACKSON — Attorney General Lynn Fitch is urging Mississippi lawmakers to pass legislation that would increase penalties for certain firearm-related crimes involving minors and crack down on gang activity across the state.

Earlier this month, Fitch called on the Legislature to approve House Bill 1165 and Senate Bill 2710. The measures would give circuit courts original jurisdiction over certain firearm-related offenses committed by minors, increase penalties for the transfer of stolen firearms and create a new criminal penalty for shooting a firearm into a group.

The proposals have been endorsed by the Mississippi Prosecutors Association, the Mississippi Sheriffs’ Association and the Mississippi Association of Chiefs of Police.

Fitch said the legislation is aimed at addressing gang recruitment of minors and ensuring penalties match the severity of the crimes.

“There are more than 200 gangs in Mississippi, and they recruit minors to do a lot of their dirty work, knowing that punishment is often disproportionate to the severity of the crime,” Fitch said in a newsletter to supporters.

She said the bills would make juvenile recruitment less attractive to gangs and help remove violent offenders from the streets.

Lawmakers are considering the measures during the current legislative session.

item.title: DARKHORSE DOGS: From abandoned mall to looking for her forever home: Nala's ready for her next chapter - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
A dog once found living near an abandoned mall in Jackson is now looking for a permanent home after a turnaround that rescue volunteers describe as a “comeback” story.

Cheshire Abbey Animal Rescue said Nala, a young dog estimated to be about 2 years old, was initially wary and protective when she was first brought into care. Volunteers said she had been living outdoors and had recently cared for puppies, all of whom have since been adopted.

With time and consistent handling, the rescue said Nala has shown a calmer, affectionate side. She enjoys playing with other dogs to burn off energy but also settles in as a “cuddlebug,” according to the rescue.

Nala weighs about 50 to 55 pounds, is spayed and is up to date on vaccinations, the group said. Cheshire Abbey noted that, because of her rough start, Nala may need patience and a slow adjustment period as she settles into a new home.

Those interested in meeting or adopting Nala can contact Cheshire Abbey through its social media channels for adoption information.

item.title: Woman charged after man shot at apartment complex in Greenville - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
GREENVILLE – A Greenville woman has been charged after police said a man was shot during a domestic dispute Tuesday at Riverwalk Apartments.

Officers with the Greenville Police Department responded Feb. 17 to a report of a shooting at 738 W. Union St., police said. When officers arrived, they made contact with a female inside the residence, who told them a male victim was in the bathroom with a gunshot wound.

The victim was transported to Delta Health System for treatment. Police said additional details about the circumstances surrounding the shooting remain under investigation.

Investigators charged 28-year-old Larineisha Askew of Greenville with one count of domestic aggravated assault. She is awaiting her initial appearance, police said.

All suspects are presumed innocent unless proven guilty.

item.title: Tate County Sheriff’s Office K-9s Soul and Stuccie receive donated body armor - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
SENATOBIA — The Tate County Sheriff’s Office said its K-9s, Soul and Stuccie, have received new bullet- and stab-protective vests through a donation arranged by the nonprofit Vested Interest in K9s Inc.

The sheriff’s office said the vests were sponsored by Don and Misty Fike of Galesburg, Illinois, and embroidered with the message: “Born to Love- Trained to Serve-Loyal Always.”

Vested Interest in K9s Inc., founded in 2009, is a 501(c)(3) organization that provides protective vests and other assistance to law enforcement K-9s nationwide. The group said the vests are U.S.-made, custom-fitted and certified to National Institute of Justice standards.

Since its founding, the nonprofit said it has provided more than 6,225 vests valued at about $6.9 million to K-9s in all 50 states through private and corporate donations.

The organization said its program is open to U.S. law enforcement dogs that are at least 20 months old and actively employed and certified, and K-9s with expired vests may also be eligible.

item.title: Study: Absolutely nobody checked on 12,659 Mississippi seniors in January - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Therese Apel
item.description:
JACKSON — Nearly one-third of Mississippi seniors who live alone went the entire month of January without a single meaningful check-in, underscoring concerns about what health officials have called a growing loneliness epidemic in America.

A new survey by Choice Mutual found that 32% of Mississippi residents age 65 and older who live alone reported no one had checked on them since Jan. 1. That equates to an estimated 12,659 seniors statewide who experienced a full month without meaningful social contact.

The findings come as national health experts warn that chronic loneliness can carry health risks comparable to smoking heavily. U.S. leaders, including the former U.S. surgeon general, have described social isolation as a public health crisis, particularly among older Americans.

Choice Mutual surveyed 3,025 seniors nationwide at the end of January, asking a single question: “Has anyone checked in on you since January 1?”

Overall, 26% said no, and Mississippi’s rate exceeded the national average.

The survey identified the five states with the highest share of seniors living alone who reported zero check-ins in January: Wyoming and Oklahoma at 50%, followed by Louisiana at 44%, and Arizona and Connecticut at 42%.

Beyond the headline number, the survey painted a broader picture of winter isolation.

Among seniors living alone nationwide:

The emotional toll appears to run deep. Sixty-eight percent said they feel forgotten or invisible during the winter. Nearly three in 10, or 29%, said they do not have even one person they can confide in during difficult times.

More than half, 58%, said they worry their loneliness is harming their health, and 62% reported that isolation affected their physical or mental health this winter.

Even seniors who do not live alone are not immune. The survey found that 65% of older adults who live with a partner still sometimes feel alone.

Anthony Martin, founder of Choice Mutual , said the results should be viewed as a warning.

“Loneliness is often dismissed as a soft issue, but the numbers show it’s anything but,” Martin said. “When a quarter of seniors go a full month without a check-in, that’s not just a social concern — it’s a public-health warning light.”

He said many seniors are not seeking major interventions, but simple connection.

“In many cases, a simple phone call or knock on the door would make a meaningful difference,” Martin said.

The survey also highlighted how difficult it can be for seniors to break the cycle of isolation. While 28% said they are very likely to reach out when feeling lonely, 40% said they are unlikely or very unlikely to do so.

Still, 64% said their well-being would improve if someone reached out more often.

As winter continues and social isolation remains a concern nationwide, advocates say small acts of connection — a call, a visit or a check-in — may be among the most powerful tools to combat what many now consider one of the country’s most overlooked health threats.

item.title: Street sweeper driver killed in collision with 18-wheeler in Lowndes County - Darkhorse Press
item.author: Morgan Howard
item.description:
LOWNDES COUNTY — The driver of a street sweeper was killed Wednesday afternoon in a crash involving an 18-wheeler on Industrial Park Road, according to the Lowndes County Sheriff’s Office.

Deputies responded to the crash at about 3 p.m. Feb. 18, 2026. A preliminary investigation indicates the street sweeper driver was fatally injured in the collision, authorities said.

The sheriff’s office said the 18-wheeler driver was not reported to have suffered life-threatening injuries.

Emergency responders and investigators remained at the scene for several hours while the crash was investigated and the roadway was cleared. Traffic on Industrial Park Road was temporarily affected.

The identity of the person killed is being withheld pending notification of next of kin.

The crash remains under investigation. Anyone with information is asked to contact the Lowndes County Sheriff’s Office.

“Our thoughts and prayers are with the victim’s family during this difficult time,” Sheriff Eddie Hawkins said in a statement. “We ask motorists to remain cautious and attentive, especially around commercial and maintenance vehicles operating on our roadways.”
